title: Shopify Built AI Merchandising Into Admin: What It Means for the Apps You Pay For
desc: Shopify Summer '26 builds AI merchandising, A/B testing, and analytics into admin. How to decide native vs the paid apps you already run on your store.
keywords: shopify ai merchandising, shopify native apps, ai collection sorting, predictive cross-sell, shopify app stack 2026, shopify summer 26 editions
cta: Want help auditing your Shopify app stack?
body:
June 17, 2026. Buried inside Shopify's Summer '26 Editions is a quieter shift that will hit some merchants' budgets directly. Shopify has moved merchandising, testing, and analytics jobs into the core admin, the same jobs a category of paid apps has charged a monthly fee to do. This is platform absorption, and it changes the question from which app do I buy to does the native tool clear my bar before I keep paying for one.

What is now native

  1. AI collection sorting that orders products by real-time conversion probability, built into admin per Shopify's developer changelog.
  2. Predictive cross-sell blocks that suggest cart upsells without a separate app.
  3. Native A/B testing for themes and checkout, plus native analytics charts, replacing tools merchants used to rent from the App Store.
  4. A merchandising insights panel for decisions that used to need a separate reporting add-on.

What it means for operators

Do not cancel anything on day one. Native tools tend to start broad and shallow, while specialist apps often keep an edge on configurability and reporting depth for a release or two. The disciplined play is a controlled test: run the native tool on a single collection or flow, benchmark it against your current app on conversion and average order value, and keep the subscription only if it still beats native on the metrics that move revenue. The savings math is the part most coverage skips. A store paying for a separate collection-sort app and a cross-sell app can carry well over a thousand dollars a year in subscriptions for jobs Shopify may now do natively, so the test is worth running deliberately rather than putting off. Some feature names were reported ahead of today's showcase, so confirm exactly what is in your admin before you rip anything out. The bigger habit is to re-justify your app budget every Editions cycle, because Shopify keeps pulling the layer above it into the core. If you want help auditing what to keep and what to cut, that is part of our Shopify app and ecommerce work, and our Shopify experts can run the native-versus-paid tests for you. For the full release and the migration deadlines that come with it, see our Shopify AI commerce playbook.

One second-order effect is worth flagging. Native AI sorting changes how your collection pages get ordered and surfaced, which can interact with how those pages are crawled and indexed. Pair any rollout with a quick crawlability check rather than assuming the old behavior holds, especially if organic search sends meaningful traffic to your category pages.
faqs: What native merchandising tools did Shopify add in Summer '26?
Shopify's Summer '26 release adds AI collection sorting, predictive cross-sell blocks, native A/B testing, native analytics charts, and a merchandising insights panel, all built into admin.
Should I cancel my Shopify merchandising apps?
Not immediately. Pilot the native tool against your current app on conversion and average order value, and keep the paid app only if it still beats native on the metrics that move revenue.
Are these features on every Shopify plan?

The native AI merchandising, A/B testing, and analytics tools reach Standard and Plus stores. Deep checkout customization for the information, shipping, and payment steps stays a Shopify Plus capability.
How do I decide native versus a paid app?

Run a clean A/B test on one collection or flow, compare conversion and average order value against your current app, and re-evaluate the decision every Editions cycle as Shopify expands native features.
